title: What Makes HFTs Tick? Tick Size Changes and Information Advantage in a Market with Fast and Slow Traders
abstract: We study the impact of two changes in the minimum tick size, a reduction and a subsequent increase, on the trading behavior of fast and slow traders in the spot foreign exchange market. We find that the most notable impact of the tick size reduction is a substantial increase in the liquidity demand of high-frequency traders (HFTs) and not the decrease in their liquidity provision discussed by prior literature. We show that this change in behavior is linked to the higher frequency of price signals that arises with the smaller tick size and to the ability of fast traders to profit from it, often at the detriment of slower traders. Following the tick size decrease and the increase in liquidity demand by HFTs in the spot market, the role of the spot market in price discovery drops relative to that of the futures market. We discuss these findings in the context of the impact of HFTs on the information content of financial markets.

author.Alain P. Chaboud: Federal Reserve Board of Governors, Washington, District of Columbia 20551;
author.Clara Vega: Federal Reserve Board of Governors, Washington, District of Columbia 20551;
author.Filip Zikes: Federal Reserve Board of Governors, Washington, District of Columbia 20551;

article.1. Introduction: Empirical studies have long confirmed that changes in trading rules on exchanges and trading platforms can have a substantial impact on the behavior of market participants, which then affects the functioning and the overall quality of financial markets. In particular, research has shown that changes in the minimum price increment, the “tick size,” influence the behavior of traders and affect the liquidity of financial markets. Nevertheless, the impact of changes in the tick size in modern financial markets, especially in the many markets where fast and slow traders coexist, is not yet fully understood, as evidenced by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)’s tick size pilot study conducted in the U.S. equity market from 2016 through 2018 (SEC 2015,Albuquerque et al. 2020) and by the discussion surrounding the recent proposal to allow for lower tick sizes on nonpenny stocks in the U.S. equity market (SEC 2022). In that context, we focus on the impact of two tick size changes in the foreign exchange (FX) market, specifically on Electronic Broking Services (EBS), an important electronic trading platform at the core of price discovery in the spot market for the two most traded exchange rates, euro-dollar and dollar-yen. In March 2011, EBS, a central limit order book used around the globe by banks and nonbanks, decreased its tick size substantially, adding an additional decimal to the quoted price, only to partially reverse that change 18 months later. Using high-frequency data for the euro-dollar and dollar-yen exchange rates that allow us to precisely observe the transactions of the three categories of market participants active on EBS, manual traders, bank algos, and nonbank algos (HFTs), we begin our analysis by documenting how these changes in tick size lead to changes in the liquidity supply and liquidity demand of these market participants. We find that when the tick size is reduced the most notable change is a substantial increase in liquidity demand by HFTs, whereas changes in liquidity supply by the various types of market participants are modest. This large increase in liquidity demand is novel in the literature because the vast majority of the prior theoretical and empirical literature has focused on how the tick size affects the liquidity supply of market participants and not on its potential effect on liquidity demand (Chordia et al. 2013,Werner et al. 2015,Yao and Ye 2015,Mahmoodzadeh and Gencay 2017,Yao and Ye 2018,O’Hara et al. 2019). To understand what drives the increase in liquidity demand, we study the impact that the tick size changes have on a measure of trading profits for the different types of market participants. Consistent with the increase in HFT liquidity demand, we document that the profits of HFT liquidity demanders increase most after the reduction in tick size, and we show that the increase in profits is linked to the increased frequency of quote updates under the smaller tick size. The increase in the number of quote updates appears to give HFTs an advantage, as they are able to process and react aggressively to price signals faster than other market participants. As our data allow us to compute a measure of liquidity demand profits separately for each combination of maker and taker types, we document that liquidity demand profits are highest for transactions where the liquidity demand comes from HFTs and the liquidity supply from slower traders, particularly in the period after the tick size decreases. This has the flavor of “sniping,” where faster traders use their speed advantage to hit existing quotes before slower traders are able to absorb and react to the new market information.1Taken together, the evidence strongly suggests that HFT liquidity demand increases after the decline in tick size because the speed advantage of HFT liquidity demanders allows them to benefit from the higher number of periods of short-lived informational advantage associated with the arrival of new price signals. Having documented how tick size changes affect the behavior and profits of different market participants, we study how the tick size changes affect market liquidity. Because traditional liquidity measures based on top-of-the book information are mechanically affected by the tick size changes, we look at several other measures to assess liquidity. The estimated price impact of net liquidity-demanding orders each minute, a comprehensive measure of liquidity that combines quantity and price information, shows only a modest reaction to the tick size changes. Volume-weighted effective spreads show some improvements in liquidity when the tick size is smaller. But cumulative depth indicates that liquidity declines somewhat with the smaller tick size. We conclude that, although the mixed evidence indicates that there is no large change in market liquidity associated with the tick size changes, market participants that trade in small sizes may face more favorable liquidity conditions with the smaller tick size, whereas market participants who trade in larger sizes may face more challenging conditions. Finally, we study how the changes we observe on EBS impact the location of price discovery in the global foreign exchange market, specifically how they affect the relative importance of the spot and futures markets for price discovery. The tick size did not change in the relevant foreign exchange futures market (the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME)) at the times of the policy changes in the spot market. Using a structural VAR framework in the tradition ofHasbrouck (1991)to estimate the information share of orders in the spot and futures market, which captures how informative aggregate order flow is for long-run price movements, we find that the informativeness of order flow in the spot market decreases substantially following the drop in tick size and the corresponding increase in HFT liquidity demand. At the same time, the informativeness of order flow in the futures market increases. After the subsequent (partial) reversal of the tick size, the role of the spot market in price discovery briefly increases again, although not enough to recover its previously dominant role. We discuss how these results likely reflect a difference between banks and HFTs in the type of information that they rely on to guide their trading activity in the FX market. Overall, our study paints the picture of a large financial market where an equilibrium between slow traders and fast traders is disrupted by a decrease in the tick size. However, the mechanism that generates that disruption, a large increase in the liquidity demand of HFTs, is not a mechanism that has received much attention in the tick size literature, a literature that has almost exclusively studied equity markets. We highlight some differences between the FX and equity markets that may explain why the strategies of HFTs in the FX market could differ from the strategies of HFTs in the equity market. This includes the market-making obligations that HFTs face in a number of stock exchanges and the difference between the two markets in access to customer order flow. In particular, the total lack of access to customer order flow by HFTs in the FX market helps explain why the role of the spot market in price discovery declines when the share of liquidity demand by HFTs in that market increases. However, we argue that our study clearly shows the need for regulators and researchers to focus more often on both the supply of liquidity and the demand for liquidity when studying the potential impact of changes in the tick size in modern financial markets. The paper proceeds as follows. InSection 2, we briefly discuss some of the related literature.Section 3offers a short description of the structure of the market and introduces the data used in this study. InSection 4, we document the impact of the tick size changes on the liquidity demand and liquidity provision of different types of market participants. InSection 5, we study the profitability of trades by the various types of participant under the different tick-size regimes and relate the profitability to changes brought about by the variation in tick size, thereby providing an explanation for the observed changes in liquidity demand by HFTs. InSection 6, we investigate how the tick size changes and the associated changes in HFT liquidity demand impact market liquidity and the location of price discovery. InSection 7, we discuss the potential role of several differences between the FX market and the equity market in explaining some of our findings. We conclude inSection 8.
article.2. Related Literature: This paper relates to two different areas of research. The first area examines the impact of various tick sizes on market functioning, including a few recent papers on how the tick size may affect HFT participation.2The second area of research, more broadly, examines the role and impact of high-frequency trading in financial markets, including what drives the behavior of HFTs and how HFTs may impact the information content of markets. Harris (1991,1994,1996,1997) highlights the role of the tick size in influencing liquidity through its effects on the incentives for market participants to provide liquidity, an issue addressed theoretically inChordia and Subrahmanyam (1995),Seppi (1997),Anshuman and Kalay (1998),Cordella and Foucault (1999),Foucault et al. (2005),Goettler et al. (2005), andKadan (2006), among others. These early papers obviously do not specifically address the trading activity of HFT market participants as they mostly predate widespread HFT activity, but our results also often support their predictions. More recently,Chordia et al. (2013),Werner et al. (2015),Yao and Ye (2015),Mahmoodzadeh and Gencay (2017),Yao and Ye (2018), andO’Hara et al. (2019)do consider the trading activity of HFT market participants in the context of tick-size changes.Chordia et al. (2013)argue that HFT liquidity providers may profit from a smaller tick size by more easily stepping in front of slower liquidity providers. In contrast,Yao and Ye (2015),Yao and Ye (2018), andO’Hara et al. (2019)discuss how a larger tick size is beneficial to HFT liquidity providers.3Broadly speaking, the logic underlying this latter finding is that a large and binding tick size does not allow liquidity providers to compete as much on prices, and therefore speed becomes a determining factor in who ends up at the top of the order book and has an opportunity to trade. Conversely, of course, this would predict that, with a reduction in the tick size, the liquidity provision of HFTs should decrease relative to the liquidity provision of slower traders. Although our empirical work does not contradict these predictions, we only observe very modest changes in HFT liquidity provision after the tick size changes on EBS. Importantly, the papers cited previously, as well as essentially all the empirical literature on the subject (Ahn et al. 1996,Bacidore 1997,Goldstein and Kavajecz 2000,Jones and Lipson 2001,Ronen and Weaver 2001,Bacidore et al. 2003,Bessembinder 2003,Coughenour and Harris 2004), focus on how the tick size affects the liquidity supply of different types of market participants. Our paper is an outlier in this literature as, driven by what we observe in the data, we focus on the impact of the tick size on the liquidity demand of market participants. In addition, almost all the empirical literature on the subject studies the equity market, with very few exceptions.Mahmoodzadeh and Gencay (2017)study the foreign exchange market, andFleming et al. (2024)study a tick size reduction in the U.S. Treasury market.4 Our paper also relates to the literature that evaluates the impact of HFT participation in financial markets. Broadly speaking, early empirical studies often discuss how an increase in algorithmic or HFT participation can improve market liquidity (Hendershott et al. 2011) and price efficiency (Brogaard et al. 2014,Chaboud et al. 2014). Somewhat in contrast to the early empirical studies, some of the subsequent literature seems to focus more on how HFTs can have a negative impact on market quality and welfare. For example,Biais et al. (2015),Foucault et al. (2016),Budish et al. (2015)andFoucault et al. (2017), among others, discuss how HFTs can reduce market liquidity due to increased adverse selection costs. Most recentlyBaldauf and Mollner (2022)discuss how faster traders are able to exploit information events by quickly adjusting their liquidity supply, andAquilina et al. (2022)show precisely how HFTs profit from latency arbitrage at the detriment of other traders via both liquidity demand and liquidity supply.
article.3. Market Structure and Data Description: 3.1. Foreign Exchange Market and the EBS Platform The market for spot foreign exchange is an over-the-counter market where market participants use a wide variety of trading venues, including single-dealer electronic platforms, multidealer electronic platforms, voice brokers, and even just telephone conversations. However, two “interdealer” trading platforms, both central limit order books (CLOBs), are at the core of price discovery for the global foreign exchange market: EBS Market and Refinitiv FX Matching (formerly Thomson Reuters Matching). Together, these two platforms are often called the “primary” FX market. In this part of the market, euro-dollar and dollar-yen, the two exchange rates with the highest global trading volume and the subjects of our study, trade primarily on EBS.5The price-discovery process for these two exchange rates is therefore concentrated on that platform, and prices on EBS are widely viewed as the reference for dealer quotes and derivatives pricing in those exchange rates.6 Access to the EBS platform is limited to foreign exchange dealing banks and under the auspices of dealing banks (via prime brokerage arrangements), to some nonbanks, primarily high-frequency trading firms and a few hedge funds and commodity trading advisers (CTAs). The origins of the platform help explain this arrangement. EBS was launched in 1993 by a consortium of the world’s largest FX dealing banks and was initially accessible only to banks and only via manual trading, using a keyboard on a dedicated terminal. Access via an API (so “computer trading”) began in 2004, and nonbanks were first allowed to trade on the platform in 2005, but only if prime brokered by a dealing bank active on the platform and if using the API. In 2006, the bank consortium sold EBS to ICAP, a financial services firm specializing in broking services and the operation of electronic markets. ICAP owned EBS at the time of the two tick size changes.7 As we highlight in the data section, one can classify the traders on EBS into three broad categories, the manual traders, all associated with banks, and two types of computer traders, banks and nonbanks. The vast majority of the nonbank trading volume comes from proprietary trading firms using low-latency trading strategies, so called HFTs. Importantly, unlike the banks trading manually and algorithmically on EBS, these HFT firms do not have access to customer order flow coming, for instance, from asset managers or nonfinancial corporations involved in international trade. There is also no payment for order flow in the FX market, so the HFTs cannot buy customer order flow from banks. In addition, in contrast with a number of HFTs active on important stock exchanges, HFTs in the FX market do not have market-making obligations. 3.2. Two Changes in Tick Size On March 7, 2011, EBS allowed the euro-dollar and dollar-yen currency pairs to be quoted with an additional decimal place. This tick size change, a decimalization, reduced the minimum price variation from one “pip” (0.0001 in euro-dollar and 0.01 in dollar-yen) to a “decimal pip” (0.00001 and 0.001, respectively). For example, say that bid and ask quotes for euro-dollar in the EBS system were 1.3645 and 1.3646 prior to the tick size change. After the tick size change, the bid and ask quotes could now be, for example, 1.36451 and 1.36459.8On September 24, 2012, about 18 months after decimalization, EBS partially reversed the reduction in tick size, now limiting the last decimal place to be either a zero or a five. This second change increased the minimum price variation from a “decimal pip” (0.00001 for euro-dollar and 0.001 for dollar-yen) to a “half pip” (0.00005 and 0.005, respectively). The two tick size changes were implemented on Monday morning (Sydney time) as the EBS system woke up from its regular weekend downtime. The rule changes and implementation dates had been announced to market participants well in advance. To see how binding the minimum tick size was around these changes, we report inTable 1the distribution of the bid-ask spreads that prevailed at the time of transactions. The first two columns of the table show that prior to decimalization, the 1-pip tick size appears to be binding about 80% of the time for both currencies, whereas after decimalization, the bid-ask spread equals the minimum tick size of 0.1 pip only about 5% of the time. The last two columns ofTable 1show that following the subsequent increase in the minimum tick size to 0.5 pip, the new tick size appears to be binding 50 to 60% of the time. We drop transactions in a four-hour period starting at 4 p.m. and ending at 8 p.m. ET. 3.3. Data Description To analyze the impact of the decrease in tick size, in 2011, and the subsequent increase, in 2012, we use high-frequency EBS data from January 2010 to December 2013. Throughout the analysis, we exclude data sampled between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m. ET, the least active trading hours of the day.9For the study, we use detailed data on both quotes and transactions. The quote data provide the first 10 levels of bid quotes and the first 10 levels of ask quotes, as well as the depth available at each of these 20 levels. All quotes are executable, and therefore the top of the book represents the true market price at that instant. The quote data are sampled every 100 milliseconds, providing a snapshot of the limit order book at that frequency. This was the highest frequency of price and order book update transmitted to EBS customers at the times of the changes in tick size, so it reflects the best information available to traders on the platform at the time of these events.10 The transactions data, recorded with millisecond precision, provide detailed information on both the volume and the direction of each trade. The minimum trade and quote size is 1 million euros for the euro-dollar currency pair and 1 million dollars for the dollar-yen currency pair. The direction of trade is based on actual trading records: A trade is recorded as, for instance, buyer-initiated, if it is the result of a “hit” on a posted ask quote. Importantly, we also observe the type of maker and the type of taker for each trade using the three categories mentioned previously, which are described later. Traders on EBS can enter trading instructions manually, using an EBS keyboard, or, on approval, via a computer directly interfacing with the system. EBS records whether a trade is placed by a keyboard interface or by an API, allowing for a classification of trades into “human” or “computer” trades. This classification into manual (human) and algorithmic (computer) trading formed the basis for the study byChaboud et al. (2014). The EBS data used in this paper now break down the algorithmic market participants into two separate categories, referred by EBS as Bank-AI and PTC-AI (AI stands for Automated Interface, and PTC stands for Professional Trading Community). As mentioned previously, the PTC-AI participants must access the system under a prime-brokerage arrangement with a dealing bank, whereas the Bank-AI participants are dealing banks trading algorithmically on their own account. Because all nonbanks are required to be prime-brokered onto the EBS system, and the vast majority of the PTC trading volume (well over 90%) comes from high-frequency trading firms, this allows us to break down the EBS market participants into three types: the manual traders (M), the Bank-AI (B), and the PTC-AI (H), with the letter H chosen to represent HFT firms. For simplicity, we also refer to these three types of market participants as manual traders (M), bank algos (B), and HFTs (H). We can now classify each transaction in our data by the type of maker (supplying liquidity) and the type of taker (consuming liquidity). Because we have three possible types of makers and three possible types of takers, we can break down the trades into nine possible combinations, MM, MB, MH, BM, BB, BH, HM, HB, and HH, where the first letter indicates who is supplying liquidity and the second letter indicates who is demanding liquidity. For example, a BH trade indicates that a bank algo is supplying liquidity (had a limit order resting in the order book) and that an HFT is demanding liquidity (submitted a market order that resulted in a trade with that resting limit order).11
article.4. Impact of Tick Size Changes on Liquid: We first analyze the impact of the two tick size changes on the shares of liquidity demand and supply of the three types of market participants. 4.1. Impact on the Liquidity Demand Shares of Various Types of Traders To study the impact of the decrease in tick size and of the subsequent increase on the liquidity demand of manual traders, bank algos, and HFTs in the euro-dollar and dollar-yen markets, we calculate for each exchange rate the following liquidity demand shares: Figure 1(a)shows monthly averages of these liquidity demand shares surrounding the two tick size changes on the EBS platform. Following the tick size decline in March 2011, HFT liquidity demand shares increase for both exchange rates, rising from about 40% up to a peak close to 60% a few months later. The share of liquidity demand coming from manual traders declines by a broadly similar amount (a bit less), whereas the liquidity demand share of bank algos is less affected. Statistical tests (not shown) of the impact of the decline in tick size on the daily liquidity demand shares of manual traders and HFTs in a 120-business-day window centered on the date of the decline show a clear statistical significance in both exchange rates, even after controlling for a linear time-trend and exchange rate implied volatility. Following the smaller increase in tick size in September 2012,Figure 1(a)shows a drop (a partial reversal) in the share of liquidity demand coming from HFTs in both exchange rates, but the immediate impact is more modest, especially in euro-dollar, and it disappears in less than a year. The movements in the liquidity demand shares of manual traders at that time, although positive in both exchange rates, as expected, are small and rather short-lived. Again, statistical tests (not shown) of the impact of the increase in tick size on the daily liquidity demand shares of HFTs in a 120-business-day window centered on the date of the increase in tick size show statistical significance in both exchange rates. In summary, there is clear evidence that the decrease in tick size from 1 pip to 0.1 pip is associated with a substantial increase in the liquidity demand of HFTs. Part of this increase in liquidity demand is reversed when EBS later increases the tick size from 0.1 pip to 0.5 pip. Next, we study the liquidity supply behavior of the various types of market participants around the two tick-size events. 4.2. Impact on the Liquidity Supply Shares of Various Types of Traders To analyze the impact of the two changes in tick size on the liquidity provision of the different types of market participants in the euro-dollar and dollar-yen markets, we calculate for each exchange rate the following liquidity supply shares: InFigure 1(b), we show monthly averages of these liquidity supply shares surrounding the two tick size changes. In contrast to the liquidity demand shares, the figure does not reveal a clear pattern of changes in liquidity supply at the times of the two tick-size events. This is confirmed by the lack of statistical significance of most of the changes in daily liquidity supply shares in tests similar to the ones conducted for liquidity demand. In both exchange rates, there is a gradual decline over the full sample in the liquidity provision shares of manual and HFT traders, and a more substantial gradual increase in the share of liquidity provision provided by bank algos, but no visible reactions to the tick-size changes. In summary, using this simple analysis, the transaction data broken down by trader type allow us to observe that the most notable reactions to the reduction in tick size are the substantial changes in the shares of liquidity demand by HFTs (an increase) and manual traders (a decrease). We do not observe a substantial impact on liquidity supply. In the next section, we discuss what may have driven these changes in liquidity demand shares, focusing particularly on the interaction between fast and slow traders. We begin by studying the profitability of liquidity demand around the changes in tick size, first averaged across all trades and then broken down by types of market participants.
article.5. Impact of Tick Size Changes on Liquid: 5.1. Average Liquidity Demand Profits We now investigate the effect of the tick size changes on the profitability of liquidity demand, beginning with broad averages across all transactions. To be clear, we do not observe actual profits realized on each trade by individual market participants, as our data do not identify individual market participants. Instead, we calculate potential profits for each trade based on subsequent price movements, further breaking down that potential profit into the effective spread and the price impact, as described later. This then allows us to study how the tick size changes affect the profitability of liquidity demand by type of trader and across the nine possible maker-taker pairs. We define potential liquidity demand profits for theith trade, which occurs at timet, as InTable 2, we show for both exchange rates five-second liquidity demand profits (negative realized spreads) averaged over all trades in 60-business-day windows before and after the tick size decline and before and after the subsequent tick-size increase. The table shows that, averaged over all trades, liquidity demand profits rise in both exchange rates after the decrease in tick size and decline after the tick size increases again. This is fully expected, as a lower bid-ask spread should reduce the cost of liquidity demand. That reduction in the cost of liquidity demand is also evident inTable 2, which shows effective spreads averaged over the same 60-business-day windows. Next, we investigate how the reactions to the tick-size changes of effective spreads, price impacts, and, especially, liquidity demand profits vary by type of trader and across the various combinations of maker and taker types, which is critical to our study. 5.2. Liquidity Demand Profits by Maker-Taker Pairs We now estimate liquidity demand profits, effective spreads, and price impacts separately for each maker-taker pair. The liquidity demand profits are calculated for the nine possible maker-taker pairs and are shown grouped by type of maker. The results inTable 3confirm what was shown in the previous section, when all trades were aggregated: With a few exceptions, liquidity demand profits rise in both exchange rates across maker-taker pairs after the decrease in tick size and decline after the tick size increases again. The new results, however, show that differences in liquidity demand profitability across maker-taker pairs are substantial. The faster traders (HFTs and bank algos) have the largest average liquidity demand (taker) profits in each subperiod considered (columns (1), (2), (4), and (5)). HFTs obtain the highest average liquidity demand profits against other types of makers, followed by bank algos, and then manual traders. As a matter of fact, in all four subperiods and in both exchange rates, the average liquidity demand profits of HFTs are positive against all types of counterparties. Conversely, in all the cases when manual traders demand liquidity, their average profits are negative. In both exchange rates the highest liquidity demand profits occur when HFTs take liquidity from manual traders, notably reaching their peak in the subperiod that follows the decrease in tick size (the top number in column (2) for each exchange rate). This peak in liquidity demand profits is, of course, consistent with the sharp increase in liquidity demand by HFTs that we observe following the decrease in tick size, as documented inSection 4.1. The analysis of average effective spreads and price impacts broken down by maker-taker pairs also reveals notable results. InTable 4, we show that, as expected, when the tick size decreases, the average effective spreads broken down by maker-taker pairs also decrease in both exchange rates for most types of trades, with the only exceptions coming in a few cases where manual traders demand liquidity. The changes in average effective spreads at the time of the increase in tick size are smaller, and, in contrast, the direction of the changes is not always fully consistent with the tick size increase. This is also not unexpected, as we showed that the tick size was binding much of the time before the tick size decrease but was almost never binding before the subsequent increase. Again, the most striking results come from looking at the differences in effective spreads between different maker-taker pairs. Recall that, in a trade, the liquidity demander (taker) aims to pay the smallest effective spread possible, whereas the liquidity supplier (maker) hopes to obtain the largest effective spread. The results show that for both exchange rates and in the four subperiods we study, that is before and after each tick-size change, when dealing with each different type of maker, the ordering of average effective spreads is always similar: HFT takers pay the smallest effective spreads, followed, in that order, by bank algos and manual traders. In addition, and by far, the largest drops in average effective spreads at the time of the tick-size decrease are associated with trades where HFTs demand liquidity, especially the trades where HFTs take liquidity from manual makers. This is again consistent with HFTs deriving much of the benefit from the smaller tick size, often at the expense of manual traders. DoesTable 5reveal a similar pattern for the price impact, in the sense that, after the tick-size decrease, the trades where HFTs demand liquidity are now followed by a larger movement in midprice in a direction that boosts profitability? The answer is a resounding no. On the contrary, for both exchange rates, following the reduction in tick size the liquidity demanding trades of HFTs see the largest decline in price impacts of the various maker-taker pairs. Therefore, the observed increase in liquidity demand profitability by HFTs after the tick size decline is due to the substantial reduction in effective spreads, particularly when HFTs are taking liquidity offered by manual traders and not to an increased success in accurately predicting price movements over the next few seconds. To a large extent, as the increase in profitability is now based mostly on what happens instantly at the moment of the trade and less on what happens over the next few seconds, this has the flavor of “sniping.” Indeed, the increase in profits comes mostly from trading on the quotes of slower counterparties and definitely not on an improved ability to predict exchange rate movements. The findings are also consistent with a strategy where the algorithms used by HFTs decide whether to enter into a liquidity demanding trade by comparing the cost of entering into that trade (represented by the effective spread) with the probability that the price will move sufficiently in a beneficial direction over the next few seconds. With a lower entry cost into each trade, the algorithms can now decide to trade aggressively even when the probability of a beneficial price movement in the next few seconds is lower. 5.3. Proportion of Profitable Liquidity Demand Trades Having studied the average profits of the various combinations of makers and takers, we now briefly report how the proportion of profitable trades for each maker-taker pair is affected by the changes in tick size. Besides being a useful concept for the work that follows, this can also be viewed as a robustness check on the previous results that considered average profits.14We now simply define a trade as profitable for a liquidity demander if it yields a strictly positive profit, as defined previously. In other words, for a liquidity-demander buying the base currency, we define a trade as profitable if, after five seconds, the midprice is strictly higher than the purchase price. To test how the tick-size changes affect the proportion of profitable trades for different maker-taker pairs, for each transaction we create a binary variable equal to one (success) if the liquidity demand profit is strictly greater than zero. Using data from 60-business-day windows before and after each change in the tick size,Table 6shows that, for both euro-dollar and dollar-yen, the proportion of profitable trades rises for all maker-taker pairs after the tick-size decline, as would be expected. Importantly, we note that both the rates of success in each subperiod and the largest increases in success rates around the decline in tick size are for trades where HFTs are taking liquidity from slower traders, especially manual traders. No maker-taker pair shows a fraction of profitable trades above 50% before the tick size decline. However, strikingly, HFTs are able to achieve that benchmark against all types of counterparties in both exchange rates in the period that immediately follows the decline in tick size, whereas banks algos can reach that threshold (barely) during that period only against manual traders in euro-dollar-trading. Based on our definition, the success rate of manual liquidity demanders remains below 50% against all types of counterparties and in all subperiods. Liquidity Demand Profits and the Frequency of Quote Updates We now study how faster market participants likely benefit from a smaller tick size. In short, we will argue that the smaller tick size leads to an increase in the frequency of quote updates, information that fast traders are then better able to exploit via liquidity demand than slower traders. This is closely related to an argument made byHaldane (2011)and developed byO’Hara (2015).16Haldane (2011, p. 6)states that “being informed used to mean being smarter than the average bear about the path of future fundamentals–profits, interest rates, order flow and the like. Adverse selection risk meant someone having a better informed view on these fundamentals. Adverse selection risk today has taken on a different shape. In a high-speed, co-located world, being informed means seeing and acting on market prices sooner that competitors.” In line with that argument, we advance that, because the lower tick-size brings about a higher number of quote updates, it is associated with an increase in the number of (very short) time periods when faster trader have a de facto information advantage because of their ability to more quickly assimilate and respond to changes in prices. In particular, we show inTable 7that the decrease in tick size leads to a large increase in the frequency of quote updates at the top of the limit order book. The observed increase in the frequency of quote updates is consistent with the prediction ofHarris (1994)that, as price improvements become less expensive, traders will submit limit orders more frequently.17Counting quote updates independently for the bid and ask sides (so one quote update if there is a change from the previous 100-millisecond reading in either the bid price or the ask price, and two quote updates if both prices change),Table 7shows that after the reduction in tick size the frequency of quote updates per second at the top of the order book increases substantially, by a factor of about three in euro-dollar and of about five in dollar-yen. The liquidity demand of HFTs is also more closely related to smaller quoted bid-ask spreads, again boosting profits. Manual traders, in contrast, show much less ability to operate profitably in a small tick-size environment where prices change often. These findings well explain our earlier observations, especially the substantial divergence in the liquidity demands of HFTs and of manual traders that follows the tick-size decline, as was shown inFigure 1(a). 5.5. Are the HFTs Really the Aggressors? The term “liquidity provision” we used in the paper implies that a market participant posting a limit order is similar to a market maker who is regularly willing to trade at the bid and/or at the ask side of the market. However, in a limit-order-book market such as EBS, any market participant has the ability to submit a limit order, which gives traders an opportunity to execute at a better price relative to crossing the spread, although obviously with less certainty of execution. In that vein,Li et al. (2021)show theoretically how automated execution algorithms can post aggressive limit orders inside the prevailing bid-ask spread, with the goal to stimulate a response from liquidity consumers. These orders, which they call “stimulating limit orders,” are clearly not primarily designed to provide liquidity to the market but to execute at a better price. Consistent with this, the report fromBIS (2020, p. 50)on FX execution algorithms notes that some algorithms used in the FX market are “passive execution strategies that are aimed at executing on the passive side of the bid-ask spread, with the intent of minimising spread cost.” The possibility that this type of trading strategy may be frequently deployed by some traders in the EBS market has important implications for the interpretation of our results. If these strategies are prevalent, then the increase in liquidity demand by HFTs that we observe after decimalization could be instead driven by an increase in the frequency of stimulating trades, that is, by the activity of traders that are recorded as liquidity suppliers in the data. Our results would then speak more to the role of liquidity supply rather than demand. The prevalence of this “stimulating” behavior cannot be perfectly measured in our data because we do not observe the market participants’ intentions. However, we are able to provide some statistics that can help us quantify how frequent this behavior could be during our sample period and therefore whether it may substantially affect our results and their interpretation. The “stimulating equilibrium” described inLi et al. (2021)involves aggressive limit orders that are immediately filled by HFTs. Because they are so aggressively placed, these limit orders do not rest in the order book, and in their theoretical model, the book looks exactly the same right before and right after these trades. Empirically, we aim to identify these “stimulating” trades by first focusing on the subset of trades in our data that satisfy this condition, that is, trades where the prevailing quoted prices and depth are exactly the same as the quoted prices and depth 100 milliseconds later (because we do not observe quotes continuously, there is likely some measurement error in our replication of the model). For the EUR/USD and USD/JPY currency pairs, respectively, 13% and 22% of all trades meet that condition. Within this subset of trades, we then look at the prices at which trades occur relative to the bid, ask, and midquotes. As defined byLi et al. (2021), when the trader recorded as an aggressor buys the base currency (so the trader using a stimulating limit order is selling the base currency), the stimulating trade occurs between the bid and the midquote. When the aggressor sells the base currency, the stimulating trade occurs between the ask and the midquote. InTable 10, we report the percentage of the trades that fulfill these conditions, before and after the decrease in tick size, and broken down by liquidity consumer type. We do indeed observe that such stimulating trades increase after the decline in tick size for all liquidity consumer types, especially for HFTs. When expressed in percent of all trades that occur inside identical prices and depth, Panel A shows an increase of the potentially stimulating trades with HFT-takers from 0.4% to 11.4% for EUR/USD and from 1.5 to 11.7% for the USD/JPY currency pair when the HFTs buy the base currency. However, as shown in Panel B ofTable 10, when expressed as a percentage of all trades, not just the ones occurring inside identical prices and depth, the magnitude of the increase is small. For instance, for trades where the HFTs buy the base currency, the percentage increases from 0.0% to 1.0% for EUR/USD and from 0.2% to 1.6% for USD/JPY. Therefore, although we believe this interesting mechanism is present in our data, blurring the distinction between liquidity provision and supply, and even more so between the aggressive and passive parties to a trade, it does not seem to be a very frequent phenomenon, at least not in 2011 and 2012, the years of the tick size changes we study. Market Liquidity and Price Discovery: In this section, we first study how the two changes in tick size in the spot FX market affect market liquidity, concluding that the effect does not seem very large. We then show that the decrease in tick size in the spot market coincides with a reduction in the importance of the spot market in price discovery relative to the futures market. 6.1. Market Liquidity Evaluating the impact a tick size change has on market liquidity is not trivial because a decrease in tick size mechanically reduces bid-ask spreads and top of the book market depth if, prior to the change, bid-ask spreads are constrained by the tick size at least some of the time. This is discussed, for instance, byHarris (1991,1994). Consistent with the predictions ofHarris (1991,1994), we show inFigure 2that the average quoted spreads in the spot market (the solid lines) decrease from almost 1.2 pip to near 0.6 pip in both exchange rates immediately after the decline in the tick size from 1 pip to 0.1 pip. The dash lines show that, in contrast, quoted spreads are little changed in the futures market for the same exchange rate, where the tick size remains at a full pip at that time.20The predicted drop in top of the book depth after the tick-size decline is evident inFigure 3, as average top of the book depth in the spot market (the solid line) drops substantially. These changes in spread and depth occur, asHarris (1991,1994) explains, because prior to the tick size decline bid-ask spreads are constrained by the tick size much of the time (Table 1). With the constraint relaxed, liquidity providers have an incentive to undercut other liquidity providers, as the smaller tick size makes it less costly to do so. A smaller tick size therefore leads to reduced bid-ask spreads. However, it also makes liquidity provision at the top of the book “riskier,” leading liquidity providers to reduce the quantity they are offering to trade. Following the increase in tick size, as shown in the lower panels of both figures, the drops in quoted spreads and top of the book depth are then both partially reversed. The observed movements in quoted spreads and depth at top of the book appear to be somewhat contradictory. Market liquidity may improve with a smaller tick size, but given the lower depth, only for an investor trading in a small size. Therefore, we first measure market liquidity using Kyle’s lambda (1985), the estimated price impact of net signed trading volumes from the point of view of liquidity consumers (often called “order flows”), which we estimate via linear regressions using data taken at one-minute intervals. This liquidity measure reflects both bid-ask spreads and depth, at the top of the book and beyond. As such, it is likely to be a more accurate reflection of true market liquidity under most circumstances compared with the traditional measures using only top of the book information. As other useful measures of market liquidity, we also estimate cumulative depth as the average of the bid and ask side depth 3-half pips away from the midpoint, as well as volume-weighted effective spreads.21 Table 11shows the three liquidity measures in the spot and futures market before and after the two tick size changes, as well as the differences between the two periods. It also reports differences in differences between the two markets for each liquidity measure. Focusing on Kyle’s lambda, we see a small decrease both in EUR/USD and in USD/JPY in the spot market after the smaller tick size is implemented (differences shown in column 3), signifying a small improvement in market liquidity, but only the reading in EUR/USD is statistically significant. However, that measure also drops slightly after the smaller increase in tick size (differences shown in column 8), again only significantly in EUR/USD. These small changes are evident inFigure 4, which shows the daily estimated one-minute price impact coefficients of net signed orders in the spot market (solid line) and in the futures market (dash line), where the tick size did not change. Using the two time series, we formally test the hypothesis of no change in market liquidity in the spot market using a difference in difference regression specification. The results, shown inTable 11, now suggest that the improvement in liquidity in EUR/USD noted after the decrease in tick size was not statistically significant (column 4) but that the improvement in liquidity in USD/JPY was significant. These results are robust to controlling for a time trend and implied volatility (not shown). In this case, the direction of the changes in market liquidity in the spot market does not differ when we look at these measures in the spot market alone or when we conduct the analysis using a comparison with movements in liquidity in the futures market. Price Discovery in the Spot and Futures Markets InSection 4.1, we showed that the decline in tick size on EBS is followed by a substantial increase in the share of liquidity demand by HFTs and a corresponding decrease in the share of liquidity demand originating with manual traders. We now investigate how this affects price discovery in the global foreign exchange market, more specifically how it affects the relative importance of the spot and futures markets in price discovery. Similar to several other major financial markets, including the markets for U.S. equities and Treasury securities (Dobrev and Schaumburg 2018), there is a substantial amount of trading activity in the foreign exchange market in both the spot and the futures markets, with important traders often active simultaneously on EBS and the CME. The prices in the two markets are very closely synchronized, and both spot and futures contribute to price discovery.22 To study the effect of the tick size change on the relative importance of the spot and futures markets for price discovery in the foreign exchange market, we first estimate the evolution over time of the information shares of spot and futures market order flows, the net signed trading volumes from the point of view of liquidity demanders. We then focus specifically on how the tick size changes and the resulting changes in HFT behavior affect the information shares of spot and futures order flows. We estimate the information shares of spot and futures order flow using the classic framework of the structural VAR ofHasbrouck (1991). This framework allows one to determine which transactions, those taking place in the futures market or those taking place in the spot market, have a more permanent impact on prices and thus are more “informative,” that is, contribute more to the long-run variance of the return process. The figure shows that both order flows contribute to the price discovery process. At the beginning of the sample the information share of spot market order flow is generally higher than that of futures market order flow. However, in 2011, when the tick size decreases from 1 pip to 0.1 pip in the spot market and remains constant in the futures market, the information share of spot market order flow decreases, whereas the information share of futures market order flow increases, which reverses the relative roles of the two markets in price discovery for both exchange rates. When the tick-size decline is partially reversed, in 2012, there is little change in the information shares. The change in relative information shares is not explained by changes in the relative trading volumes of the two markets. If anything, although monthly trading volumes are quite variable, there seems to be a slight increase in the difference in trading volumes between the spot and futures markets after the tick size decline. 6.3. Discussion of the Price Discovery Results The degradation of price discovery in the spot market associated with the decrease in tick size may appear to be at odds with the decrease in tick size leading to a higher number of quote updates, which could increase the amount of information in the market and may thus improve price discovery. Our interpretation is instead that the more frequent quote updates help predict mostly short-lived price movements, while other sources of information such as end-user order flow (Menkhoff et al. 2016) or macroeconomic information (Brandt and Kavajecz 2004,Green 2004,Pasquariello and Vega 2007) help predict longer-lived price movements. Furthermore, as we explain later, different market participants specialize in strategies with different time horizons, with each type of market participant profiting from its own comparative advantage. Specifically,Menkhoff et al. (2016)show that foreign exchange dealers, the bank algos, and manual traders in our data set have access to end-user order flow, which in turn contains fundamental economic information that drives long-term exchange rate changes. In contrast, the HFTs in the FX market, without access to that private information, tend to specialize in exploiting the short-lived price movements that can be predicted by rapidly analyzing the public market data that is released every 100 milliseconds. To explore this hypothesis further, we estimate Kyle’s lambda, the price impact of aggressive orders, at different time horizons ranging from 100 milliseconds to 1 minute, for the different types of market participants. Figures 7and8plot our estimates and shows two facts that further support our interpretation of the results. First, aggressive HFT order flow has the largest impact on prices of all types of order flow at the shortest horizon, but bank algo and manual order flows have a larger impact on prices at longer horizons. Notably, the estimated Kyle lambdas for HFTs decline gradually as the horizon increases, whereas they remain broadly flat for Manual and Bank AI traders. Second, aggressive HFT order flow has a lower impact on prices after the decrease in tick size, whereas the aggressive order flow of manual and bank algo traders are about as equally informative before and after the tick size change. This aligns well with our finding that the decrease in tick size mainly improved the ability of aggressive HFTs to minimize the effective spread, whereas their ability to predict future price movements beyond a very short horizon declined. These two facts support our interpretation of the changes in information shares we observe in our analysis of the spot and futures markets. The information shares we estimate are meant to capture fundamental economic information about long-term exchange rate changes (the horizon we consider is 30 minutes). Thus, the decrease in the information share of the aggregate spot-market order flow that we observe after the decrease in tick size is consistent with the increase in the share of aggressive HFT trading activity, activity that focuses mostly on the very short horizon, including minimizing the effective spread. Because we find a contemporaneous increase in the informativeness of the market order flow in the futures market, our finding that the spot market produces relatively less information postdecimalization does not necessarily mean that the FX market as a whole produces less information.26Furthermore, it is also possible that the informativeness of limit orders in the spot market increases after the decrease in tick size, as quote updates become more frequent.Chaboud et al. (2021)study the information content of limit orders in the EBS market between 2008 and 2017 and do indeed document a small increase in the informativeness of limit orders in the EUR/USD and USD/JPY currency pairs after decimalization. That increase, however, is clearly not large enough to fully offset the decrease in the informativeness of the spot market order flow documented in this paper. Finally, on a less formal level, conversations with market participants confirm that slower traders broadly viewed the change to a smaller tick size at the time as having provided an additional advantage to HFTs on the EBS platform, disturbing a precarious equilibrium in a market where fast and slow traders had learned to coexist. In simple terms, slow traders may have been willing to accept a certain level of adverse selection and order anticipation by HFTs in return for the improved market liquidity that the presence of HFTs often provided. Following the decline in tick size, the increase in HFT liquidity consumption without a contemporaneous improvement in market liquidity upended that equilibrium, and the role of the spot market in price discovery suffered.27The decision by EBS, 18 months later, to move to a larger tick size, was an attempt to address that issue.
article.7. FX Market and Equity Market: Some Imp: Our analysis leaves little doubt that the changes in tick size on EBS, especially the large decline in tick size, had a substantial impact on this important sector of the foreign exchange market. As we discussed, the most notable effect observed after the decline in tick size is a large increase in the liquidity demand of HFTs. At the same time, the impact on the liquidity supply of the various types of market participants, the primary focus of almost all the tick size literature, is modest. Because that literature has almost exclusively studied the effect of the tick size on equity markets, it is natural to ask whether some of the differences between the foreign exchange and the equity markets may help explain some of our findings. In particular, as we focus on the behavior of HFTs, considering the differences between the HFTs active in each of these markets is important.28As mentioned previously, in many important stock exchanges across the world, a number of HFTs face market-making obligations. Therefore, studying how their market-making behavior is affected by the tick size is entirely logical. In contrast, in the FX market, HFTs do not face market-making obligations, and they can implement any mix of algorithmic strategies, passive or aggressive, that they choose. In addition, the fact that HFTs on EBS have to be prime-brokered onto the platform by bank dealers whose primary activity on EBS is market-making may well lead these HFTs to focus more on strategies that do not directly compete with their prime brokers. Accentuating this observed difference, in some of the equity market studies we cited, the sample of HFTs is composed exclusively of firms with at least some market-making obligations, whereas HFTs without market-making obligations are also active in the market but are not studied. That is the case, for instance, inO’Hara et al. (2019, p. 54), where the authors are careful to label the sample of HFTs they study as “HFT market makers” and state that “Activity by high-frequency trading firms that do not make markets on the NYSE may potentially appear as part of the ‘quantitative‘ category, but we do not have exact identification of HFT strategies other than those of the DMMs (Designated Market Makers) and SLPs (Supplemental Liquidity Providers).” Therefore, besides some genuine differences between the HFT strategies used in the two markets, it is also possible that the “unconstrained” HFTs in the equity market, those that are more likely to follow liquidity-consuming strategies similar to what we observe in the FX market, have been underrepresented in some of the empirical studies. Another relevant difference between HFTs in the FX market and in the equity market relates to the type of information they have access to. As discussed previously, HFTs in the FX market do not have access to customer order flow information, such as order flow from asset managers that banks observe through their trading activity with these firms. This is important, as we mentioned, because that customer order flow is likely to be helpful in predicting long-term exchange rate movements. In contrast, in the equity market some HFT firms have access to customer order flow, as they may be asked by financial firms to execute customer orders or may even pay to gain access to and execute customer orders. There are also clear differences in the access to price and trade information between the over-the-counter FX market and the exchange-based equity market. For instance, there is nothing in the FX market similar to the consolidated tape that exists in the U.S. equity market. Despite its shortcomings, the tape very likely provides a better dissemination of price and trade information than exists in the FX market. This may make the price and depth information that HFTs can acquire by trading on EBS more valuable and easier to exploit than similar information in the equity market. This fact, in addition to the lack of access to customer order flow by HFTs described above, may also help explain both the increased focus of HFTs in FX on quote updates and the low explanatory power of HFT order flow for long-term price movements which our analysis has uncovered. Overall, it is likely that some of the results we obtain in our analysis of the FX market are indeed due, at least in part, to features peculiar to that market. However, we believe that our study also shows that the impact of the tick size on the liquidity demand of HFTs in other markets where fast and slow traders coexist, including the equity market, clearly deserves some increased attention, particularly as HFT firms increase the variety of strategies they use in these markets.
article.8. Conclusion: We study the impact on the behavior of fast and slow traders of two tick size changes in the spot foreign exchange market: a decrease and a subsequent partial reversal. Using detailed high-frequency trading data in the two most-traded exchange rates, euro-dollar and dollar-yen, we show how the changes in tick size affect the trading activity of three different groups of market participants: manual traders, bank algos, and HFTs. The most salient finding is that the tick size reduction is associated with a sizeable increase in the share of liquidity demand by HFTs. In contrast, we find only a modest change in liquidity supply, which has been the focus of the previous literature. We show that this increase in liquidity demand by HFTs, and the increased profitability of those trades, is tied to the substantial increase in the frequency of quote updates that occurs after the tick size is reduced. Although HFTs are able to exploit the more frequent arrival of new market information, this often comes at the detriment of the slowest traders in the market, the manual traders. In particular, the frequency of trades consistent with the “sniping” of manual traders by HFTs increases. We find that these changes in tick size in the spot foreign exchange market do not lead to notable changes in market liquidity. However, following the decline in tick size and the resulting increase in HFT liquidity demand, the role of the spot market in price discovery drops relative to that of the corresponding futures market, which experiences no change in tick size at that time. This likely reveals that the order flow emanating from the increased HFT activity in the spot market contains less information pertinent to the long-run efficient price of exchange rates than the order flow from slower traders that it replaces. Together, the findings in this paper clearly illustrate the need for a balanced ecology in financial markets where fast and slow traders coexist, an ecology that can be importantly affected by market design and trading rules. The findings also highlight the need for regulators and researchers to focus more often on both the supply of liquidity and the demand for liquidity when studying the potential impact of changes in the tick size in modern financial markets.
